artiushin-2026-spider-atlas.abstractA: We present a three-dimensional immunofluorescence atlas of the synganglion of the hackled-orb weaver spider Uloborus diversus. Whole-mount synapsin staining registered to a common reference volume was combined with antibodies against GABA, acetylcholine (ChAT), serotonin (5-HT), octopamine/tyramine (TDC2), and neuropeptides including allatostatin A, CCAP, FMRFamide, and proctolin. The atlas resolves transmitter architecture across leg, opisthosomal, pedipalpal, and cheliceral neuropils. Leg neuropils show stereotyped innervation across all four pairs. The arcuate body is organized into four sublayers with differential transmitter content. Mushroom bodies retain the complete haft-body-head form and are exclusively AstA-positive, with cholinergic and GABAergic globuli cells. We identify two previously uncharacterized protocerebral structures: a tonsillar neuropil with a serotonin core and TDC2 shell, and a candidate protocerebral bridge with layered GABAergic, octopaminergic, proctolinergic, and cholinergic architecture. These structures are proposed as components of a spider central complex homolog.
artiushin-2026-spider-atlas.abstractB: Understanding spider brain organization requires systematic mapping of neurotransmitter distributions across identified neuropils. We constructed a three-dimensional immunofluorescence atlas of the Uloborus diversus synganglion by registering whole-mount confocal volumes stained for synapsin and eight neurotransmitter markers to a common reference. Because all primary claims are observational anatomical facts whose evidence is the atlas itself, reproduction requires re-registering deposited volumes rather than re-computing statistics. The atlas reveals a four-sublayer arcuate body whose layers are discriminable only through multi-transmitter co-staining, not synapsin alone. Two novel protocerebral structures — a tonsillar neuropil and a candidate protocerebral bridge — display compartmentalized transmitter architectures consistent with central-complex homologs, reframing orb-weaver neuroanatomy within the broader arthropod central-complex lineage. GAD antibody penetration was limited to the preparation periphery, constraining GABA mapping in deep neuropils. These findings nominate specific structures as substrates for web-building behavior in a non-cursorial species.
artiushin-2026-spider-atlas.comparison: Abstract A covers more neuropil regions and transmitter details; Abstract B sacrifices breadth to develop the central-complex homology argument and acknowledge the GAD antibody limitation.

bouyeure-2026-fear-rsa.abstractA: We used representational similarity analysis on fMRI data across fear acquisition, reversal, and test phases to investigate how the brain encodes and updates threat representations. During acquisition, threatening CS+ cues produced generalized between-item representations in dACC, SFG, caudate, and insula (CS type F=479.35, p<0.0001). During reversal, newly dangerous CS-+ cues acquired generalized fear-network patterns while changing-contingency cues exhibited elevated item-specific stability in precuneus and IFG. Context representations became more distinct in dmPFC and lateral PFC during reversal. PFC context specificity predicted reinstatement of acquisition fear traces at test: higher specificity predicted greater CS-+ than CS+- item reinstatement in MTG (t(22)=2.51, p<0.05). During test phases, no univariate BOLD differences emerged despite significant behavioral US expectancy differences, motivating the RSA approach. These findings reveal dual representational strategies — generalization and item-specific coding — operating simultaneously across dissociable brain regions.
bouyeure-2026-fear-rsa.abstractB: How does the brain adapt threat representations when contingencies reverse? We tested three hypotheses using RSA on fMRI data: that fear acquisition produces generalized cue representations, that reversal recruits simultaneous generalization and item-specific strategies in dissociable regions, and that prefrontal context coding predicts fear renewal. Each was confirmed. During acquisition, CS+ items shared overlapping patterns in the fear network without elevated item stability, establishing generalization as the selective encoding mode. During reversal, newly dangerous cues inherited this generalized code while contingency-changing cues developed item-specific stability in precuneus and IFG. Individual differences in PFC context specificity during reversal predicted reinstatement of acquisition memories at test, implicating prefrontal context coding as a mechanism for fear renewal. RSA analyses used only unreinforced trials to avoid US-driven BOLD confounds; ROI analyses were derived from searchlight clusters on the same dataset, creating potential circularity.
bouyeure-2026-fear-rsa.comparison: Abstract A includes more statistical details and regional findings; Abstract B develops the three-hypothesis argumentative structure and notes the ROI circularity caveat.

ejdrup-2026-dopamine.abstractA: We used large-scale three-dimensional simulation of extracellular dopamine to investigate regional differences in striatal dynamics. At 4 Hz pacemaker activity, dorsal striatum produced isolated hotspots without tonic baseline while ventral striatum showed pervasive tonic-like coverage. Parameter sweeps identified DAT Vmax as the sole parameter generating this regional difference, with a 3:1 DS:VS ratio. Immunostaining confirmed elevated DAT in DS (p=0.0021); dSTORM showed greater DAT nanoclustering in VS (p=0.012). VMAT2 showed no dorsoventral gradient, ruling out differential release. D1 receptor occupancy tracked phasic dopamine with approximately 50 ms delay; D2 occupancy integrated over seconds and could not resolve brief pauses or closely spaced bursts. Dense 20 nm nanoclusters took approximately 400 ms to clear a 100 nM bolus versus 200 ms for unclustered DAT. VS was more sensitive to DAT modulation, with 50% Vmax changes shifting tonic DA by 38 nM versus 11 nM in DS.
ejdrup-2026-dopamine.abstractB: The prevailing view treats phasic and tonic dopamine as distinct release modes, but we show both emerge from a single release process filtered through region-specific DAT activity. A 3D tissue-scale simulation reproducing published FSCV data demonstrates that the dorsoventral contrast in striatal dopamine dynamics — isolated hotspots in DS versus diffuse tonic coverage in VS — is explained entirely by a threefold difference in DAT Vmax, not by release parameters. Immunostaining and super-resolution dSTORM imaging corroborate this: DAT protein is elevated in DS while DAT nanoclustering is greater in VS, with VMAT2 showing no gradient as a control for release capacity. The simulation further reveals that D1 receptors function as burst detectors while D2 receptors integrate over seconds and cannot resolve individual pauses, constraining theories assigning D2 signaling a role in rapid event-level computation. The nanoclustering and tissue-level models operate independently without formal coupling, and nanoclustering results assume constant total Vmax across conditions.
ejdrup-2026-dopamine.comparison: Abstract A packs in more quantitative details including specific p-values and effect magnitudes; Abstract B builds the argument against the phasic/tonic dichotomy and flags the model-coupling scope limitation.

gadeke-2026-guilt-insula.abstractA: Across an fMRI study (N=40) and a behavioral replication (N=44), we investigated interpersonal guilt using a within-subject lottery paradigm. Happiness decreased more following low partner outcomes when the participant chose (Social) versus the partner chose (Partner condition), regardless of own outcome (Study 1: d=0.56, BF10=32; Study 2: d=0.40, BF10=3.8 for high own outcome). A computational model with a responsibility-weighted partner-RPE term outperformed alternatives, with the social_pRPE weight reliably positive (Study 1: Z=2.85, p=0.004; Study 2: Z=3.26, p=0.001). Left anterior insula BOLD was elevated in Social relative to Partner trials after negative partner outcomes (peak MNI [-28, 24, -4]). Left STS tracked partner RPEs specifically under participant responsibility. Bilateral ventral striatum tracked expected values, validating the model-based approach. gPPI revealed condition-dependent insula-right IFG coupling. Risk premiums did not differ between Social and Solo conditions (BF10=0.17-0.49).
gadeke-2026-guilt-insula.abstractB: We tested whether anterior insula encodes interpersonal guilt as responsibility-contingent affect, whether happiness incorporates partner prediction errors weighted by agency, and whether STS represents partner state conditional on responsibility. In a lottery paradigm, happiness dropped more after partner harm when the participant was responsible, replicated across fMRI (N=40) and behavioral (N=44) studies. A computational model with social partner-RPE weights outperformed alternatives, and the fitted weight was reliably positive in both samples. Anterior insula tracked the responsibility-by-partner-outcome interaction; STS tracked partner RPEs selectively under participant agency; gPPI revealed condition-dependent insula-IFG coupling. Critically, the partner's decisions were generated by an expected-value-maximizing algorithm rather than a real person, a structural deception conditioning all social claims. Risk premiums were unaffected by social context, arguing against risk-aversion as an alternative explanation.
gadeke-2026-guilt-insula.comparison: Abstract A maximizes statistical coverage across both studies; Abstract B develops the three-hypothesis structure and foregrounds the algorithmic-partner deception as a structural caveat.

headley-2026-inhibitory-rhythms.abstractA: Using a biophysically realistic compartmental model of a layer 5 pyramidal neuron supporting Na+, NMDA, and Ca2+ dendritic spikes, we investigated how rhythmic inhibition at distinct compartments regulates neural integration. Doubling distal dendritic inhibition reduced somatic firing from 5.5 Hz to 0.2 Hz by suppressing dendritic spikes; doubling perisomatic inhibition reduced firing to 0.7 Hz by elevating AP voltage threshold. Frequency sweeps established that beta (~20 Hz) optimally entrained dendritic spike onsets at distal sites, while gamma (40-80 Hz) optimally phase-modulated perisomatic AP threshold. Beta-frequency distal inhibition gated clustered apical input; gamma-frequency perisomatic inhibition gated proximal and basal input, with the two effects remaining functionally orthogonal. Ca2+ spikes preceded somatic APs by approximately 20 ms; Na+ spikes by 2-3 ms. Perisomatic inhibition produced both subtractive and divisive effects on the input-output curve. Phase-dependent modulation emerged within the first few cycles of oscillatory bursts.
headley-2026-inhibitory-rhythms.abstractB: Parvalbumin-positive interneurons target perisomatic compartments and entrain gamma rhythms; somatostatin-positive interneurons target distal dendrites and associate with beta. We ask whether this correlation reflects a mechanistic coupling between compartment and frequency. In a biophysically detailed single-cell model, distal dendritic inhibition principally suppresses Ca2+ and NMDA dendritic spikes whose lead times (~20-25 ms) match beta cycle periods, while perisomatic inhibition modulates AP threshold at timescales matching gamma. Frequency sweeps confirm beta as optimal for distal entrainment and gamma for perisomatic modulation. The two gating regimes are functionally orthogonal: gamma perisomatic inhibition phase-modulates AP threshold without altering dendritic spike rates. This provides mechanistic grounding for the empirical PV-gamma and SST-beta associations. All results derive from a single isolated neuron without network dynamics or recurrent connectivity. The model uses approximately 26,000 excitatory and 4,500 inhibitory synapses with published parameters but no sensitivity analysis over these choices.
headley-2026-inhibitory-rhythms.comparison: Abstract A covers more quantitative results including firing rates, timing, and input-output effects; Abstract B builds the argument from the PV/SST literature to mechanistic explanation and notes the single-cell and sensitivity-analysis scope limitations.

kammer-2026-foveal-feedback.abstractA: In a gaze-contingent fMRI paradigm where peripheral saccade targets disappeared before fixation (99.27% exclusion rate), we decoded target identity from foveal V1 at 57.43% accuracy (t(27)=8.81, p<0.001). Cross-decoding from feedback to direct-stimulation conditions yielded 57.2% accuracy (t(27)=5.22, p<0.001), indicating shared representational format. Feedback decoding was weaker than direct stimulation (84.06%, t(27)=19.92, p<0.001). A U-shaped eccentricity profile in V1, V2, and V3 ruled out peripheral spillover as an explanation. Foveal V1 decoded shape but not semantic category: cross-category decoding dropped significantly (t(27)=2.25, p=0.033) while cross-shape decoding remained high. Lateral occipital cortex showed the inverse pattern. IPS activity was significantly more strongly associated with foveal than peripheral decoding (t(27)=2.53, p=0.026), with the relationship reversing under direct stimulation (t(27)=-3.61, p=0.004). The IPS analysis was exploratory and not preregistered.
kammer-2026-foveal-feedback.abstractB: We tested whether foveal V1 receives image-specific feedback about peripheral saccade targets, whether this feedback carries shape rather than category information, and whether it shares a representational format with direct foveal stimulation. Using a gaze-contingent paradigm that excluded targets from the fovea in 99.27% of saccades, multivariate classifiers decoded target identity from foveal V1 despite the absence of foveal input. The U-shaped eccentricity profile — high at fovea, low parafoveally, high peripherally — rules out spillover from large receptive fields, which would predict monotonic decay. Cross-condition decoding confirmed a shared representational format between feedback and bottom-up stimulation. The feedback preserved shape but not semantic category, while lateral occipital cortex showed the inverse pattern, constraining the signal to low-to-mid-level visual content. IPS emerged as a candidate driver in an exploratory analysis, but this was not preregistered and the preregistration itself was submitted to OSF only after manuscript submission.
kammer-2026-foveal-feedback.comparison: Abstract A includes all key statistics and decoding accuracies; Abstract B develops the argumentative chain from hypothesis to evidence and foregrounds the preregistration timing caveat.

kolb-2026-igabasnfr2.abstractA: We engineered iGABASnFR2, a next-generation GABA sensor, by screening 3,947 variants from 39 targeted sites near the Pf622 binding pocket and cpGFP linkers in primary cultured neurons. iGABASnFR2 achieves 4.1-fold higher delta-F/F, 13.1-fold more responsive pixels, 7-fold higher on-cell affinity (EC50 6.4 micromolar), and 30% faster rise time (43 versus 61 ms) compared to iGABASnFR1, with high selectivity for GABA. A negative-going variant, iGABASnFR2n, provides -2.2-fold delta-F/F. A 2.60 angstrom crystal structure (PDB 9D57) shows cpGFP remains rigid upon GABA binding. Side-by-side comparisons demonstrate single-bouton hippocampal GABA detection where v1 yielded no signal, single-trial direction-selective retinal GABA release from starburst amacrine cells, and volume-transmitted GABA in somatosensory cortex following whisker stimulation (approximately 2-2.5 micromolar peak). Both v2 sensors show single-exponential stopped-flow kinetics, unlike v1's biphasic kinetics.
kolb-2026-igabasnfr2.abstractB: We tested whether near-saturation mutagenesis could yield a GABA sensor crossing qualitative capability thresholds — enabling measurements that iGABASnFR1 cannot make at all, not merely improving existing ones. Screening 3,947 variants identified iGABASnFR2 with 4.1-fold improved sensitivity and 7-fold higher affinity within the physiologically relevant range above tonic extracellular GABA concentrations. Three demanding preparations confirmed the capability threshold was crossed: iGABASnFR2 detected single-bouton hippocampal GABA release where v1 produced no signal across 15 trials in five experiments; resolved direction-selective starburst amacrine cell release on single trials where v1 failed even after trial-averaging; and detected volume-transmitted cortical GABA in vivo. A negative-going variant provides independent corroboration of positive-going signals, addressing recurrent concerns about intensiometric sensor artifacts. The crystal structure reveals that cpGFP remains conformationally rigid upon GABA binding, contrasting with the GCaMP mechanism. All biological demonstrations use standard laboratory mouse and rat preparations.
kolb-2026-igabasnfr2.comparison: Abstract A maximizes quantitative coverage of sensor properties; Abstract B structures the argument around the capability-threshold hypothesis and addresses the artifact-corroboration rationale for the negative-going variant.

meijer-2025-serotonin-additive-r1.abstractA: Using brain-wide Neuropixels recordings of 7,478 neurons across 13 regions in SERT-Cre and wild-type mice, we investigated whether phasic dorsal-raphe serotonin acts additively or multiplicatively. Optogenetic DRN stimulation modulated 10-60% of neurons per region bidirectionally, drove pupil dilation, whisking, sniffing, and suppressed hippocampal sharp-wave ripples. A per-neuron GLM yielded significant choice and stimulation main effects but a near-zero choice-by-stimulation interaction (-0.05 plus-or-minus 0.06; t(9)=-0.92, p=0.38), confirming additive modulation and eliminating multiplicative gain control. A receptor-expression GLM showed 5-HT1a and 5-HT2 receptors contributed oppositely signed effects explaining roughly half the variance in regional modulation. The 5-HT effect axis in population PCA space was significantly more orthogonal to the choice axis than shuffle nulls, with orthogonality growing toward the choice moment. Decision behavior was completely unaffected: psychometrics, reaction times, prior updating, and choice-model parameters remained intact, refuting the surprise-coding hypothesis.
meijer-2025-serotonin-additive-r1.abstractB: Neuromodulators are conventionally modeled as multiplicative gain controllers since Servan-Schreiber (1990), but we show serotonin acts additively brain-wide. In 7,478 neurons across 13 regions, optogenetic DRN stimulation produced robust neural modulation yet left perceptual decision behavior entirely intact. The critical test was a per-neuron GLM: significant main effects of choice and stimulation, but a near-zero interaction term, eliminating multiplicative gain as the brain-wide mode of 5-HT action. This additive architecture geometrically entails that 5-HT modulation occupies a population subspace orthogonal to choice coding — demoting orthogonality from an independent finding to a consequence of additivity. A receptor-expression model shows 5-HT1a-mediated baseline suppression dominates over 5-HT2A-mediated gain, reconciling the two prior receptor-specific pictures. The behavioral null result refutes surprise coding. All conclusions are scoped by the use of synchronous SERT-Cre optogenetic drive, which is broader and more uniform than endogenous mixed-selectivity DRN ensemble activity.
meijer-2025-serotonin-additive-r1.comparison: Abstract A covers more empirical details including state-switch effects and receptor variance explained; Abstract B builds the argument from overturning the gain-control framework and clarifies orthogonality as a geometric consequence rather than independent finding.

meijer-2025-serotonin-orthogonal.abstractA: We investigated phasic dorsal-raphe serotonin function using Neuropixels recordings of 7,478 neurons across 13 regions in 17 mice. Optogenetic 5-HT stimulation in SERT-Cre mice dilated the pupil, suppressed hippocampal sharp-wave ripples, and increased whisking and sniffing during quiet wakefulness, confirming a state-switch hypothesis. Brain-wide, 10-60% of neurons per region were modulated bidirectionally, with modulated fraction scaling with ChR2 expression. Suppression appeared at short latencies (~25 ms in hippocampus); a fast-spiking interneuron mechanism was ruled out. Despite this widespread modulation, the same stimulation during a steering-wheel perceptual task left psychometric slope, bias, lapse rate, reaction time, prior updating, and all choice-model coefficients unchanged, refuting a surprise-coding hypothesis. In population PCA space, the 5-HT modulation axis was significantly more orthogonal to the choice axis than block-aware shuffles, with orthogonality growing toward the choice moment and reaching per-region significance in mPFC, visual cortex, and supplementary motor area.
meijer-2025-serotonin-orthogonal.abstractB: Serotonin modulates neurons throughout the brain, yet its impact on ongoing behavior remains unclear. We tested three hypotheses: that phasic 5-HT drives an offline-to-online state switch, that it codes surprise to alter perceptual decisions, and that it occupies a neural subspace orthogonal to choice coding. The state-switch hypothesis was confirmed — optogenetic DRN stimulation produced pupil dilation, ripple suppression, and exploratory behavior against wild-type controls. The surprise hypothesis was refuted — the same stimulation left every behavioral metric and choice-model parameter unchanged despite modulating 10-60% of neurons per region. This dissociation motivated the orthogonal-subspace hypothesis: 5-HT modulation and choice coding occupy geometrically orthogonal population dimensions, enabling neuromodulation to reshape activity without perturbing downstream readout. Both predictions — near-orthogonality and growing orthogonality toward the choice moment — were confirmed. All claims are scoped by the caveat that optogenetic activation is broader and more uniform than endogenous serotonin release.
meijer-2025-serotonin-orthogonal.comparison: Abstract A includes more quantitative neural details including latency structure and regional significance; Abstract B develops the three-hypothesis narrative arc from confirmation through refutation to resolution.

rozak-2026-neurovascular-dl.abstractA: We developed NOVAS3D, a deep-learning pipeline combining UNet/UNETR segmentation, longitudinal registration, boundary-detection radius estimation, and graph-theoretic analysis for volumetric two-photon neurovascular imaging. The ensemble outperformed ilastik on Dice, precision, and HD95 (p<0.05); registration nearly doubled tracked vessel count (241 to 412 per field); radius estimation recovered simulated ground truth at R-squared=0.68. Applied to Thy1-ChR2 cortex, the pipeline revealed baseline within-vessel radius heterogeneity of 24 plus-or-minus 28%, capillary dilations occurring closer to labeled neurons than constrictions (16.1 versus 21.9 micrometers, p<1e-4), dilators biased toward the cortical surface (37 plus-or-minus 179 micrometers shallower), network assortativity increasing 152 plus-or-minus 65%, and a 4% median gain in capillary network efficiency under 4.3 mW/mm-squared photostimulation. Green-light and wild-type controls showed no such effects. Segmentation generalized qualitatively to C57BL/6 mice, Fischer rats, and light-sheet microscopy without retraining.
rozak-2026-neurovascular-dl.abstractB: We tested coupled hypotheses: that a deep-learning pipeline can recover network-level neurovascular measurements unavailable to conventional approaches, and that optogenetic neuronal activation drives coordinated graph-level microvascular responses. The NOVAS3D pipeline significantly outperformed ilastik segmentation, registration nearly doubled tracked vessels, and radius estimation recovered simulated ground truth. Applied to Thy1-ChR2 cortex, within-vessel radius heterogeneity of 24% at baseline demonstrates that point measurements cannot accurately estimate microvessel volume changes. Dilations occurred closer to labeled neurons than constrictions, network assortativity rose 152%, and capillary efficiency increased 4% — all absent in green-light and wild-type controls. These network-coordination findings jointly rule out the assumption that better single-vessel segmentation can substitute for graph-level analysis of functional hyperemia. The pipeline was trained exclusively on one preparation; quantitative out-of-distribution performance is not reported. The 2-SD responder threshold is not sensitivity-tested in the main analysis, conditioning all quantitative biology claims.
rozak-2026-neurovascular-dl.comparison: Abstract A maximizes quantitative coverage of pipeline benchmarks and biological results; Abstract B develops the argument that neurovascular coupling is intrinsically network-level and foregrounds the single-preparation training and threshold sensitivity caveats.

scheller-2026-self-prioritization.abstractA: Using temporal-order-judgment tasks with hierarchical Bayesian TVA modeling across two experiments (N=69, N=71), we investigated self-prioritization in attentional selection. Self-associated stimuli were processed 1.5 Hz faster than other-associated under perceptual decisions but the advantage vanished or reversed under explicit social decoding (-1.6 Hz, HDI95: -3 to -0.26 Hz). A condition-specific capacity model decisively outperformed a weights-only model (delta-loo=14.2, weight=0.86), indicating self-association mobilizes additional processing capacity rather than redistributing attentional weights. Processing capacity increased by 2.6 Hz in the perceptual condition. For other-associated stimuli, the social-by-perceptual salience interaction was near zero (-0.54 Hz), consistent with additivity and independent mechanisms. For self-associated stimuli, the perceptual salience benefit was markedly reduced (2.5 versus 5.2 Hz for other, 6 Hz neutral), indicating sub-additivity. Individual self-prioritization effects correlated with social processing rate changes (r=0.354, BF10=8.23). Perceptual salience produced a 6 Hz advantage driven by both increased salient and decreased non-salient rates.
scheller-2026-self-prioritization.abstractB: We tested whether self-prioritization operates through capacity mobilization or weight redistribution, whether it is automatic or dependent on explicit social decoding, and whether social and perceptual salience combine independently. Self-associated stimuli were processed faster under perceptual decisions but not social decisions, establishing automaticity at early pre-attentive stages that is disrupted by active social decoding. TVA model comparison decisively favored condition-specific capacity over weights-only, demonstrating that self-association recruits additional processing resources. In Experiment 2, social and perceptual salience combined additively for other-associated stimuli (interaction indistinguishable from zero), but self-associated stimuli showed sub-additive interaction — the perceptual salience benefit halved. This partially refutes strong independence: social and perceptual salience stream independently except when self-relevance obligatorily dominates, forcing a qualified rather than unrestricted independence account. Both experiments were preregistered, and the hierarchical Bayesian framework uses deposited Stan posterior summaries. Generalization beyond arbitrary shape-label associations and TVA-based estimation is not established.
scheller-2026-self-prioritization.comparison: Abstract A packs in more numerical results across both experiments; Abstract B builds the three-hypothesis argumentative structure and develops the qualified-independence conclusion from the sub-additivity finding.

wengert-2026-kcnc1.abstractA: In a conditional Kcnc1-A421V/+ knock-in mouse modeling KCNC1 developmental and epileptic encephalopathy, cortical PV interneurons showed reduced Kv3.1 surface expression, reduced potassium current density, impaired maximal firing, and altered AP waveform (reduced downstroke velocity p=0.0012, prolonged APD50 p=0.0053 at P16-21). Excitatory neurons were unaffected at both juvenile and adult ages. Layer II-IV PV-INs showed larger deficits than layer V, consistent with differential Kv3.1:Kv3.2 expression ratios. Reticular thalamic PV neurons showed attenuated frequency-current relationships. Inhibitory synaptic transmission was intact in juveniles (P16-21) but altered in young adults (P32-42), with increased uIPSC magnitude and reduced paired-pulse ratio. In vivo, 7/7 mutant mice showed hypersynchronous cortical discharges with myoclonic seizures; excitatory cells showed increased calcium transient frequency. Eight of 12 mice exhibited spontaneous seizures (0.62 plus-or-minus 0.24/day); 4 SUDEP events were captured. All mutants died before 122 days (Mantel-Cox p<0.001). Cognitive deficits included impaired spatial learning and working memory.
wengert-2026-kcnc1.abstractB: We tested three linked hypotheses: that the recurrent KCNC1-A421V variant produces Kv3.1 loss of function through impaired trafficking, that this selectively compromises PV fast-spiking interneurons while sparing excitatory neurons, and that PV dysfunction suffices to drive network hyperexcitability and encephalopathy. In a conditional knock-in mouse, PV interneurons showed reduced Kv3.1 surface expression, reduced potassium current, and impaired fast spiking, while excitatory neurons were functionally spared at all ages. Cell-type specificity graded with Kv3.1:Kv3.2 expression: superficial PV-INs showed the largest deficits, deep-layer PV-INs milder effects confined to high current injections. Synaptic inhibition was intact in juveniles but deteriorated by young adulthood, supporting a progressive model where PV-interneuron compromise is causally upstream of the encephalopathy. Mutant mice exhibited spontaneous seizures, SUDEP, premature lethality, and cognitive deficits recapitulating human KCNC1 DEE. Conclusions require this proprietary mouse model and cannot be reproduced from deposited data alone.
wengert-2026-kcnc1.comparison: Abstract A covers more quantitative electrophysiology and in vivo results; Abstract B develops the three-hypothesis causal chain from trafficking defect through cell-type selectivity to progressive circuit failure, and notes the reproducibility scope limitation.
